Movements & Schools

Design Schools

The thinking behind each style — its founding idea and the values it represents.

Modern

1920s – 1970s

The building is a clear diagram of its purpose. Structure is expressed, space flows, and the wall becomes a thin skin. Beauty lives in proportion and the honest assembly of parts.

Form follows functionTruth to materialsFree plan & free façadeVolume over mass

Contemporary

2000s – present

Contemporary work prizes resolution over style — combining materials and geometries that suit site, climate and brief, balanced with restraint and a clear formal idea.

Material mixClean geometryContextual responseBalanced contrast

Minimalism

1980s – present

Remove everything inessential so the remaining surface, proportion and aperture can be felt fully. The empty plane is as important as the built mass; the detail is invisible perfection.

Reduction to essenceMonolithic surfacePerfection of the fewLight & shadow

Scandinavian

1950s – present

Form serves everyday life with quiet care; exteriors are calm and warm, designed around scarce northern light and the surrounding landscape.

Functional simplicityNatural materialsDaylightConnection to nature

Japanese Minimalism

1990s – present

The building negotiates gently between inside and out through layered thresholds, restrained materials and a deep sensitivity to light, season and landscape.

Harmony with natureSimplicityNatural materialsThreshold & layering

Mediterranean Contemporary

1980s – present

Clean white masses, deep shade and seamless terraces make warm-climate living gracious; the exterior is shaped by sun, breeze and the sea.

Response to sun & heatIndoor–outdoor livingThick protective wallsCourtyards & shade

Tropical Modern

1990s – present

The exterior is a climate device — open to breeze, shaded from sun and rain, dissolving into greenery so building and landscape become one.

Climate responseNatural ventilationDeep shadeIndoor–outdoor merging

Desert Modernism

1940s – 1970s

Mid-century glass-and-steel clarity is recalibrated for the desert: broad shading roofs, sun screens and breeze-blocks let the house open fully to the landscape while staying cool.

Climate-tuned modernismIndoor–outdoor flowDeep shadeFlat planes

Organic Architecture

1900s – present

The building is conceived as a living organism rooted in its place: cantilevers echo rock ledges, materials come from the land, and structure, space and ornament are one.

Harmony with siteForm from natureContinuity of inside & outTruth to materials

Biophilic

2010s – present

The façade becomes a living system — planted, watered and textured — dissolving the boundary between building and ecosystem to restore people's connection with nature.

Living nature firstGreenery & waterNatural materials & lightWellbeing

Industrial

1990s – present

Beauty is found in the unfinished and functional — steel frames, brick walls and big industrial glazing left frankly visible rather than dressed.

Exposed structureHonest materialsUtility as aestheticAdaptive reuse

High-Tech

1970s – present

By externalising structure and services, the interior is freed for flexible use and the façade becomes a legible diagram of exactly how the building works.

Structural expressionServices as ornamentFlexibility & modularityPrefabrication

Parametric

2000s – present

Architecture as a responsive system: parameters drive form so every element varies smoothly within a coherent whole; walls, floors and roofs merge into continuous surfaces.

Parametric generationContinuous differentiationFluidity & seamlessnessField & flow

Deconstructivism

1980s – present

It questions the assumed harmony of structure and form — tilting planes and fracturing volumes to create tension, surprise and frozen movement.

FragmentationNon-rectilinear geometryControlled instabilityDisruption

Brutalism

1950s – 1980s

Brutalism refuses disguise. Structure and circulation are made visible and celebrated; the building is an honest sculpted object of weight and texture.

Truth to materialsStructural honestyMonumentalityExpression of mass

Mid-Century Modern

1945 – 1970

The post-war house dissolves the boundary between exterior and landscape with glass walls, overhangs and decks, expressing a relaxed, modern way of life.

Clean linesIndoor–outdoor flowOpen planHonest structure

Prairie Style

1900 – 1920

The house lies low against the prairie: emphatic horizontal bands, deep eaves and ribbon windows tie it to the ground and the open horizon.

HorizontalityConnection to the landOpen planIntegral ornament

Neo-Classical

1750s – 1900s (revived since)

Universal rational rules of beauty inherited from antiquity: proportion governs all, the orders provide a disciplined grammar, and symmetry expresses order and civic virtue.

The classical ordersSymmetry & axialityHarmonic proportionHierarchy & decorum

Classical

Antiquity (8th c. BCE – 4th c. CE)

Beauty arises from a fixed, rational system of orders and proportions; the temple front, colonnade and pediment express civic and sacred dignity through perfect balance.

The classical ordersSymmetry & axialityHarmonic proportionMonumentality

Renaissance

15th – 16th century

Façades are composed as balanced mathematical wholes — superimposed orders, rusticated bases and serene symmetry expressing humanist confidence in reason and proportion.

Harmonic proportionSymmetry & orderClassical orders revivedHumanism

Baroque

17th – 18th century

The exterior is staged for emotional impact — walls undulate, orders break forward and back, and sculpture and light create restless, awe-inspiring movement.

Drama & movementCurving, dynamic formGrandeur & scaleRich ornament

Art Deco

1920s – 1940s

Deco disciplines decoration into geometric, machine-inspired motifs, expressing optimism and the romance of technology through controlled ornament and soaring form.

Modernity through ornamentVerticality & aspirationSymmetry & orderLuxury materials

Art Nouveau

1890 – 1910

Line itself becomes alive — curving, growing, botanical — as iron, stone and glass are sculpted into a unified organic ornament that erases the line between structure and decoration.

Nature as ornamentSinuous 'whiplash' lineUnity of art & structureNew materials (iron, glass)

Beaux-Arts

1850 – 1920

Composed by strict academic rules, the exterior projects civic grandeur through symmetry, a hierarchy of grand entrances and staircases, paired columns and abundant sculpture.

Monumental classicismAxial symmetryHierarchy & grandeurRich sculptural ornament

Victorian

1837 – 1901

The exterior delights in variety and ornament — asymmetrical picturesque massing crowned with towers and gables, every surface enriched with trim, colour and pattern.

Eclectic revivalPicturesque silhouetteDecorative exuberancePolychromy

Gothic Revival

1740 – 1900

Verticality and the pointed arch drive the exterior upward; tracery, pinnacles and rich stone carving create a romantic, aspirational, picturesque silhouette.

Pointed-arch structureVerticality & aspirationTracery & ornamentPicturesque irregularity

Romanesque Revival

1840 – 1900

Weight and permanence define the exterior — rusticated stone, deep round-arched openings and squat columns create a robust, grounded, powerful presence.

Massive masonryRound-arch structureRugged solidityBold simplicity

Colonial Revival

1880 – 1955

The exterior is calm and balanced — a symmetrical façade centred on a refined pedimented entrance, with multipane windows and modest classical trim conveying quiet dignity.

Symmetry & balanceClassical restraintTraditional materialsRefined proportion

Vernacular

Timeless / pre-modern

Form follows climate, material and custom: thick walls for heat, steep roofs for snow, courtyards for shade — a practical, evolved architecture deeply of its place.

Local materialsClimate adaptationTradition & craftPracticality

Sustainable / Passive

2000s – present

The exterior is an environmental instrument — oriented, shaded, planted and ventilated so the building works with climate, lowers energy and brings nature back into the city.

Performance-led formNatural ventilation & shadingLiving façadesRenewable materials
Architecture Atlas

A practical, exterior-focused architectural design reference — choose a direction and architectural language for any project. Built for design teams.
